RULING - The court informed Mr. Bey that he has no standing in court to represent Ms. Cornejo. He is neither a party or an attorney. The Court adopted its tentative ruling. Plaintiff Marimar Cornejo's demurrer to all of the affirmative defenses in the answer to the second amended complaint filed by defendant American National Property and Casualty Company is overruled in its entirety. The demurrer was purportedly filed by Jamil Bey as the "authorized representative" of Ms. Cornejo. However, Mr. Bey, who is not an active member of the California Bar, may not represent Ms. Cornejo absent a court order such as appointment of a guardian ad litem and no such order has been issued. Consideration of the merits of the demurrer would be tantamount to allowing unauthorized practice of law. Moreover, it appears that Mr. Bey may be seeking to relitgate prior orders of this court that he is not a proper plaintiff. Mr. Bey's remedy is to seek relief from those orders from the Court of Appeal, not unilaterally re-assert himself into this case as Ms. Cornejo's "representative. Order signed in open court. DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Dec-11-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, Marc G. Bonino of the law offices of Hayes Scott Bonino & Ellingson LLP (650-637-9100) appears as counsel for defendant/Moving Party, American National Property and Casualty Company

Attorney, Jason B. Shane of the law offices of Lombardi Loper & Conant, LLP (510-433-2600) appears as counsel for defendant/non-opposing party, Key Point Credit Union

Attorney, Dennis J. Kelly of the law offices of Dillingham & Murphy LLP (415-397-2700) appears as counsel fordefendant/non-opposing party, Southwest Business Corporation

No other appearance is made in this matter

The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

DEFENDANT AMERICAN NATIONAL PROPERTY AND CASUALTY COMPANY DEMURRER TO FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT, as to the second and fourth through twelfth causes of action in the first amended complaint filed by plaintiffs Marimar Cornejo and Jamil Bey is: a) overruled as to the second, fifth, sixth, eighth, ninth, tenth and twelfth causes of action alleged by Ms. Cornejo; b) sustained without leave to amend as to the fourth, seventh and eleventh causes of action alleged by Ms. Cornejo; and c) sustained without leave to amend as to all causes of action alleged by Mr. Bey. Some of the issues raised by this demurrer were previously addressed in the order issued by Judge Kiesselbach on the defendant Keypoint Credit Union's demurrer to the complaint and there is no persuasive reason why there should be a different result on this demurrer. As to the second, fifth, sixth, eighth, ninth, tenth and twelfth causes of action alleged by Ms. Cornejo, it cannot be determined as a matter of law that Ms. Cornejo is not a third party beneficiary of the insurance policy. The "policy" or notice of insurance that is cited is a condensed version of the master policy and therefore the actual terms of the master policy are not part of the record on this demurrer. The notice of insurance that is cited shows that the borrower (Ms. Cornejo) can submit a claim, which indicates that the parties contemplated that she should benefit from the agreement. Whether a person is an intended beneficiary is a usually a factual issue and thus not amenable on a demurrer. Ms. Cornejo's fourth cause of action alleges that ANPCC engaged in premium overbilling, which is not ground for a bad faith claim. (Jonathan Neil & Associates, Inc. v. Jones (2004) 33 Cal.4th 917, 939.) As to Ms. Cornejo's seventh and eleventh causes of action 7 and 11, her remedy is to pursue her bad faith claim regarding ANPCC's claims handling practices rather than sue for negligence or negligent misrepresentation. (Sanchez v. Lindsey Morden Claims Services (1999) 72 Cal.App.4th 249, 254.) The first amended complaint does not plead ultimate facts showing how ANPCC owed a tort or contractual duty to Mr. Bey. Paragraph 13 of the first amended complaint and the retail installment contract, which is attached to the original complaint and referenced in the amended complaint, show that only Ms. Cornejo was a party to the transaction. Written order is signed and filed in open court.

DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Oct-02-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Marimar Cornejo, In pro per (510-285-7930) appears as self-represented plaintiff/opposing party

Jamil Bey, In pro per (510-285-7930) appears as self-represented plaintiff/opposing party

Attorney, Jason B. Shane of the law offices of Lombardi, Loper & Conant, LLP (510-433-2600) appears as counsel for defendant/demurring party, Key Point Credit Union

Attorney, Dennis J. Kelly of the law offices of Dillingham & Murphy LLP (415-397-2700) appears as counsel for defendant, Southwest Business Corporation

The cause is argued and submitted by both sides as set forth in the transcript of the certified shorthand reporter.

The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

DEFENDANT KEYPOINT CREDIT UNION's DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT is OVERRULED as to plaintiff Cornejo's claims. The demurrer is OVERRULED as to causes of action 2, 3, 6, 9, 10, 11, and 12. The court cannot determine as a matter of law that Ms. Cornejo is not a third party beneficiary of the insurance policy. First, the "policy" that is cited is a condensed version and therefore the actual terms of the master policy are not presently before the court. Second, what is attached shows that the borrower (Ms. Cornejo) could submit a claim, which indicates that the parties contemplated that she should benefit from the agreement. Lastly, Ms. Cornejo correctly argues that determining whether a person is an intended beneficiary is a factual issue. (See Spinks v. Equity Residential Briarwood Apartments (2009) 171 Cal.App.4th 1004, 1025 ("Generally, it is a question of fact whether a particular third person is an intended beneficiary of a contract.").) The court overrules the demurrer as to Ms. Cornejo's causes of action 1, 4, 8, and 12. Even if Keypoint is allowed to purchase the insurance, its discretion is not unlimited. Ms. Cornejo alleges that Keypoint violated the contract by, inter alia, failing to disclose the settlement and improperly back-dating the insurance and charging her for times that had already lapsed (Complaint, pars. 59-61; 65; 120.) These allegations support the claims. Nothing in the contract necessarily authorizes charges regardless of amount. Nor does the contract clearly authorize backdating policies to cover periods of time where no loss occurred. The court also generally defers to the plaintiff's construction of the contract on demurrer. (See Aragon-Haas v. Family Security Ins. Services, Inc., (1991) 231 Cal.App.3d 232, 239.) Keypoint's demurrer to Mr. Bey's claims are sustained without leave to amend. The complaint does not plead ultimate facts showing how Keypoint owed a tort or contractual duty to him. The complaint indicates that only Ms. Cornejo was a party to the transaction (complaint, pars. 11, 12, and 118) and plaintiffs concede that point. Mr. Bey does not adduce any facts showing how he can cure the defect. The prevailing party shall submit a [proposed] written order that tracks the Court's ruling, verbatim and that complies with CRC 3.1312.
